History of Present Illness:
Pt is a 52M s/p pancreas-after kidney transplant 2004-01-15
who's course was complicated by a readmission for dirrhea and
newonset A Fib 2019-04-21.  He was successfully cardioverted
anddischarged home on coumadin.  INR was least 2.9 on 05-27.  Was
to have
repeat labs today after the holiday.

Presented to nephrology clinic today, however, with a SBP in the
80's and history of several days of very dark stools and he was
sent to the University Of Toledo Medical Center ED.  SBP 86 on arrival, but improved to 130's
with IVF.

Patient reports poor PO intake for the last 2-3 days given the
extreme heat (90's) and no A/C at home.  He has continued to
take his medications as perscribed.  Aside from feeling tired
(which
he attributed to the heat), he has no other complaints.

No HA/dizziness/CP/cough/SOB/abdominal pain. + dark stools. No
emesis.

NG lavage in the ED was reportedly "negative".  Rectal guaiac +.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Rowe is a 52 YOM s/p pancreas-after kidney transplant
2004-01-15
whose course was complicated new onset AFib requiring
cardioversion.  He was successfully cardioverted and discharged
home on coumadin 1992-12-03. He then presented to the Avera St Anthony's Hospital
Clinic on 1985-12-04 with hypotension and melena.  He was found to
have a supratherapeutic INR (>20.2) likely secondary to poor PO
intake and diarrhea.  He was admitted to the ICU by the
Transplant Surgery service and given IVF resuscitation, Vitamin
K, and FFP.  His hematocrit and coags were checked serially.  A
GI consult was obtained, however, endoscopy was not performed as
his melena was deemed to be from a mucosal bleed in the setting
of a supratherapeutic INR.  His INR trended down to therapeutic
range, and he was transferred to the floor on hospital day 3.
Both Cardiology and Hematology were consulted, and his coumadin
was restarted.

Later on hospital day #3, he spiked a fever to 101.4.  Blood and
urine cultures were sent, which returned negative.  A chest
radiograph was also clear, and no infectious source was
identified.  After 48 hours of being afebrile, he was deemed to
be appropriate for discharge.  His INR was 1.5, and he was
discharged on warfarin 1mg daily.  He was arranged to have INR
monitoring by the Banner Lassen Medical Center Clinic, with MWF lab draws.

Medications on Admission:
coumadin 5mg daily
prograf 3mg Metro Health Hospital
valcyte 450mg daily

asa 81mg daily
prednisome 5mg daily

lopressor 37.5 po BID

6. Famotidine 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q12H (every 12

7. Prednisone 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

8. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID
(2 times a day).
9. Tacrolimus 1 mg Capsule Sig: Two (2) Capsule PO Q12H (every
12 hours) for 2 doses.
10. Calcium Carbonate-Vitamin D3 600 mg(1,500mg) -400 unit
Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO once a day.


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Supertherapeutic INR (on coumadin)
Fever


Discharge Condition:
Stable/good. INR 1.5
A+Ox3
Ambulatory


Discharge Instructions:
-Please call the transplant clinic at 738-525-8233 for fever,
chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, increased
abdominal pain, pain over the graft or other concerning
symptoms.
-Monitor for signs of bleeding to include nose bleed, bright red
blood per rectum, dark/tarry stool, increased length of time to
stop bleeding, easy bruising. You should shave with an electric
razor.
-You are to have labs drawn starting on Monday 06-20 to include
INR with your regular transplant labs. Thereafter, you will need
to have labs Monday, Wednesday, and Friday to follow your INR.
Results should be faxed to Michelle Barnes at the transplant clinic
at 105-89-3338.  The transplant clinic will call you with any
dosage adjustments.
-No heavy lifting.
No driving if taking narcotic pain medication
